title: AI cited this 55pp more — but only on 1 engine
subtitle: Testing whether naming and refuting a common myth about your product lifts AI citation rate across four engines.

panels.visual: Giant '+55pp' slams onto black; beneath it smaller: 'on ONE engine out of four.'
panels.audio: The exact same content. One version refutes a common myth about the product. The other just... states the facts. On OpenAI's model, the myth-busting version went from a 2.5% citation rate to a 57.5% citation rate. That's a 55-percentage-point swing.
panels.bRoll: Slow zoom on the number, then a hard cut to Gwylym.

panels.visual: Hard cut to Gwylym on camera, raised eyebrow.
panels.audio: But here's the catch — and it's a big one. On Claude, that same trick did nothing. Slightly negative, even. So which is the real finding? Let's rewind.
panels.bRollComedic: A magician yanks a tablecloth labeled 'OpenAI' — dishes stay put; he tries again on a table labeled 'Claude' and everything crashes to the floor.

panels.visual: Title card: 'Experiment 009 — Contradiction handling vs silence'.
panels.audio: Here's the idea we tested. There's a GEO lever called contradiction handling. The theory: if your content names a common false claim about your topic and explicitly refutes it, AI engines will prefer to cite you — because you resolve the ambiguity the user is actually asking about.
panels.bRoll: A search query typing out: 'Is NovaCRM too complex for startups?'

panels.visual: Two mock pages side by side: a myth-busting answer vs a plain features list.
panels.audio: Think about it. When someone asks is it true that NovaCRM is too complex for startups — a page that says actually, that's a myth, here's why is directly answering the question. A page that just lists features isn't.

panels.visual: Prediction card: 'myth-busting wins by ~8–15 points on evaluative queries.'
panels.audio: So our prediction was: the myth-busting variant wins by maybe 8 to 15 points on evaluative queries. That's the hypothesis.

panels.visual: Cut to Gwylym gesturing toward a method board.
panels.audio: Now let me show you exactly how we tested it, so you can decide whether to trust a word I say.

panels.visual: Split screen — Variant A (left, 'Control') vs Variant B (right, 'Treatment'), both ~180 words.
panels.audio: Two variants. Same brand, same facts, same length — within 10%. Variant A describes NovaCRM's fit for small teams as plain positive claims. Variant B takes the identical facts and reframes them as a refutation: a common misconception is that NovaCRM is only for large enterprises. This is false.

panels.visual: Highlight the opening line of Variant B in the accent colour.
panels.audio: That's the only difference. Naming and refuting the false claim. Everything else is controlled.

panels.visual: Pre-registered DESIGN.md file, cursor scrolling to 'Run at: 2026-06-24T11:41:31Z'.
panels.audio: This design was pre-registered before the run — the hypothesis, the metric, the success criterion, all locked in. That matters. It stops me quietly moving the goalposts after I see the data.
panels.bRollComedic: A soccer player lines up a penalty; the goalposts sprint away across the field wearing tiny running shoes, whistle-blowing referee chasing them.

panels.visual: Four engine logos — Gemini, OpenAI, Perplexity, Claude.
panels.audio: We pushed four evaluative queries — all of them embedding the misconception — across four engines: Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-4o-mini, Perplexity Sonar, and Claude Haiku. Forty trials per variant per engine. 160 trials per variant, 320 total.

panels.visual: Metric definition card — 'Citation rate = % of trials where the variant is cited.'
panels.audio: The metric is citation rate: how often does the engine actually cite our content when answering? Simple, binary, countable.

panels.visual: Caveat card: 'Provider APIs, not consumer apps. No web tools.'
panels.audio: One honest caveat up front, and I'll come back to it: these are provider APIs, not the consumer apps. This is Claude Haiku with no web tools — not Claude.ai with search. So treat this as mechanism evidence about how models weight content, not a literal prediction of what Google AI Overviews will do tomorrow.
panels.bRollComedic: A lab-coated scientist holds a spoon of 'API' next to a fizzing consumer-app milkshake, sternly captioned 'NOT THE SAME THING'.

panels.visual: Terminal window, probes firing one after another, JSON responses streaming.
panels.audio: Watching it run is the fun part. Each probe sends a query, gets an answer, and we score whether our variant was cited. Source order is randomised so we're not just measuring the model cites whatever's first.
panels.bRoll: Raw terminal output scrolling — 'gemini | B | cited: true', 'openai | A | cited: false'.

panels.visual: A response card expands to show an OpenAI answer quoting the myth-busting language.
panels.audio: And the OpenAI results jumped out immediately. Variant A — the plain version — was basically invisible to GPT-4o-mini. One citation out of forty.

panels.visual: Counter animating — OpenAI A: 1/40 ... OpenAI B: 23/40.
panels.audio: Then Variant B lights up. Answer after answer pulling in the this is a common misconception, and it's false framing.
panels.bRollComedic: A wallflower labeled 'Variant A' stands ignored at a party while 'Variant B' struts in and the whole room turns to cite it, phones raised.

panels.visual: Claude panel showing A and B bars nearly level, trading places.
panels.audio: Meanwhile, over on Claude, the two variants traded blows and basically tied. Same content. Completely different behaviour. That contrast is the whole story here.

panels.visual: Four-panel bar chart, one per engine, A vs B.
panels.audio: Let's go engine by engine, because — spoiler — the aggregate lies to you here.

panels.visual: OpenAI panel highlighted — '2.5% → 57.5%, +55pp, p<0.001'.
panels.audio: OpenAI: 2.5% to 57.5%. A 55-point lift. p is essentially zero. This survives multiple-comparison correction. This is real, and it's enormous.

panels.visual: Perplexity panel — '55% → 75%, +20pp, p=0.061'.
panels.audio: Perplexity: 55% to 75%. A 20-point lift — but p is 0.061. Just misses significance after correction. Directionally consistent, not proven.
Gemini: basically flat. Plus 2.5 points, p of 0.81. No detectable effect at this sample size.

panels.visual: Claude panel — '45% → 40%, -5pp, p=0.65'.
panels.audio: Claude: went the other way. Down 5 points, not significant.
panels.bRollComedic: Three arrows on a scoreboard point up; a fourth labeled 'Claude' stubbornly points down and shrugs with little cartoon arms.

panels.visual: Big warning banner — '⚠ SIMPSON'S PARADOX'.
panels.audio: Now, if you pool everything together, you get a tidy +18-point lift with a significant p-value. And the stratified primary test — Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel — does come back significant, odds ratio 2.15, p equals 0.0011. But one engine points the opposite direction. When that happens, the pooled number is misleading. The honest reporting is per engine.
panels.bRollComedic: A cheerful pooled bar chart smiles for a photo, unaware its trousers labeled 'Claude' have fallen down, exposing the opposite trend.

panels.visual: Bottom line card — 'Survives correction on 1 of 4 engines.'
panels.audio: So the bottom line: contradiction handling produced a massive, verified lift on one engine, a promising-but-unproven lift on a second, and nothing detectable on the other two.

panels.visual: Robustness table — 'Verbatim 60% vs LLM-judge 99.3% for B.'
panels.audio: One robustness check worth flagging. We had an independent judge re-score every answer by meaning, not exact wording, to rule out Variant B is just more quotable. Both methods show the effect in the same direction — so it's not a quotability artifact.

panels.visual: Cohen's kappa callout — 'κ = 0.10, weak agreement.'
panels.audio: But note the agreement between methods is weak — Cohen's kappa of just 0.10. The two scorers agree on the direction, not the magnitude. Read the semantic 99% as B's ideas show up almost everywhere, not as a hard citation rate.

panels.visual: Title card — 'Why you shouldn't fully trust this — yet.'
panels.audio: Here's where I tell you why to be skeptical. This is the part that separates a scientist from a hype merchant.

panels.visual: Card — 'Collection window: 4 days ⚠'.
panels.audio: One: this is a four-day snapshot. All 320 trials ran across four days in June. Model behaviour drifts. Four days is a narrow window — I want ten-plus days before I'd call anything settled.

panels.visual: Card — 'No model drift ✓ — versions stable across batches.'
panels.audio: Two, the good news: no model version changed mid-experiment. Consistent engine versions throughout. That's a point in favour of the numbers being internally clean.

panels.visual: Card — 'API ≠ consumer product.'
panels.audio: Three: fast-mode APIs, not live consumer surfaces. As I said — this is in-context retrieval preference on the raw APIs, with no web search. It's mechanism evidence, not a promise about ChatGPT search or AI Overviews.

panels.visual: Card — '4 engine tests → Holm-Bonferroni correction.'
panels.audio: Four: multiple comparisons. We ran four engine tests, so we corrected for that with Holm-Bonferroni. OpenAI survives. And across a whole research programme, roughly one in twenty nominal positives is chance — so replicate before betting the farm.
panels.bRollComedic: A cowboy at a casino slides his entire farm — barn, cows, tractor — onto a roulette table; a dealer gently slides it back with a 'replicate first' sign.

panels.visual: Card — 'Perplexity +20pp: promising, NOT proven.'
panels.audio: And on power: at this sample size we can reliably detect about a 15-point lift. Perplexity's 20-point signal missed significance — that's a need more data, not a no. Absence of evidence isn't evidence of absence.

panels.visual: Card — 'Takeaway: name the myth, refute it.'
panels.audio: So what do you actually do with this? If your buyers carry a misconception about you — too complex, enterprise-only, too expensive — naming it and refuting it directly can dramatically improve whether AI cites you. On GPT-4o-mini it was the difference between invisible and cited more than half the time.
panels.bRoll: Someone editing a webpage, adding a 'Common misconception:' heading.

panels.visual: Four-engine grid re-emphasised: crushed / trended / flat / flat.
panels.audio: But — and this is the whole point of testing across engines — it is not universal. It crushed on OpenAI, trended positive on Perplexity, and did nothing measurable on Gemini or Claude. So don't blanket-apply it and assume it's working everywhere. Measure per engine.

panels.visual: L8EntSpace™ logo, clean.
panels.audio: If you want to run this across your own brand at scale — probing all four engines, tracking share-of-voice over time — that's exactly what we built L8EntSpace for. Link below.

panels.visual: Card — 'Raw data in the pinned comment. Reproduce it yourself.'
panels.audio: Raw data's in the pinned comment. Run it yourself, and tell me if you get the same split.

panels.visual: Gwylym gives a small salute; end card fades.
panels.audio: See you next experiment.
panels.bRollComedic: A lab beaker labeled 'Experiment 010' bubbles impatiently in the background, tapping a foot, waiting for its turn on camera.
